nativeRange.habitat: Large river channels, flooded plains, and river margins; typically inhabits open-water mid-column to bottom zones of major river systems including the Amazon, Orinoco, and Paraná basins

decorPreferences: large smooth driftwood for cover and resting
minimal fine decor to prevent ingestion
large flat rocks as bottom substrate anchors
sparse planting — robust species only (will uproot plants)
open swimming space essential for active mid-column movement

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in the wild; consumes large fish, invertebrates, and crustaceans. In captivity, can be transitioned to large whole fish (tilapia, smelt, whitebait), raw prawns, mussels, and large carnivore pellets or sticks. Live feeder fish are not recommended due to disease and nutritional imbalance risk. Juveniles may accept large bloodworm and chopped fish flesh. Feeding every 2–3 days is sufficient for large adults. Do not overfeed — water quality degrades rapidly from uneaten protein.

goodWith: other large robust Amazonian fish of similar or larger size (e.g., large Arapaima in public-aquarium settings)
large Pacu (Colossoma macropomum) in very large public-aquarium displays
avoidWith: any fish smaller than its mouth width — will be eaten
conspecifics in confined space — territorial and cannibalistic at size

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Pseudoplatystoma fasciatum; shows characteristic black tiger-stripe banding on pale grey-brown body. Check current FishBase page for updated image URL.

summary: A massive Amazonian predator reaching 90–105 cm, patterned with bold black tiger stripes on a pale grey body. Sold as an ornamental juvenile but grows rapidly into a piscivore requiring public-aquarium scale facilities. Commercial food fish throughout South America; deeply unsuitable for home aquaria.
careNotes:
The Tiger Shovelnose Catfish is one of the largest Pimelodid catfishes and one of the most frequently misrepresented species in the ornamental trade. Juveniles are sold at 5–12 cm and are striking in appearance, but the species grows rapidly — often reaching 40–50 cm within the first 12–18 months under good conditions — and ultimately attains 90–105 cm total length in the wild, with captive specimens regularly exceeding 80 cm. No home aquarium realistically houses an adult specimen; even a 3 m tank provides inadequate swimming room for a mature individual.

As a scaleless catfish (skin-only, lacking bony scutes over most of the body), Pseudoplatystoma fasciatum is highly copper-sensitive. Copper-based medications (common for ich and parasites) must be avoided entirely; alternative treatments such as heat, salt, or formalin-based treatments are required. This applies to both direct medication and incidental copper contamination from plumbing.

The species is an active piscivore and will consume any tankmate that fits within its broad, flat, shovel-shaped mouth. The mouth width often exceeds what keepers expect — fish approaching half the catfish’s own body length are at risk of predation. Escape risk is high; this species is a powerful jumper and can clear surprisingly low openings. A fully weighted, tightly fitted lid is essential.

The Tiger Shovelnose is a major commercial food fish throughout the Amazon basin and is farmed extensively in Brazil and Bolivia. It is widely available in the ornamental trade due to farming scale and low wholesale cost, which contributes to its frequent, regrettable sale to home aquarists. Hobbyists should be directed firmly toward the species’ public-aquarium-only status.
breedingNotes: Not achieved in home aquaria. Commercial aquaculture breeding uses hormone-induced spawning (carp pituitary extract injection) in large raceways and ponds. Wild spawning occurs during seasonal flood events in the Amazon basin, when fish migrate into flooded forest (igapó) areas. Eggs are pelagic and drift in open water. No parental care. Captive home breeding is not feasible given adult size and hormonal induction requirements.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudoplatystoma fasciatum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 105 cm TL), temperature range (23–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Amazon, Orinoco, Paraná basins), diet (piscivore), scaleless copper-sensitivity note, and husbandry guidance on tank size requirements.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudoplatystoma fasciatum
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 105 cm TL, commonly to 90 cm), native range (Amazon and Paraná river basins across Brazil, Bolivia, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Paraguay, Argentina), taxonomy (Pimelodidae, Siluriformes), diet (carnivore/piscivore), commercial importance as food fish. Maximum weight recorded at 35 kg.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Tiger Shovelnose Catfish
additional.notes: Trade-hobbyist perspective. Confirmed adult size (over 3 feet / ~90 cm), piscivore diet, copper sensitivity as scaleless catfish, escape risk, minimum tank recommendation for juveniles (200+ gallons), and expert care classification. Emphasized species is not suitable for typical home aquariums long-term.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Giant catfish species guide
additional.notes: Referenced for hobbyist advisory context and confirmation of public-aquarium-only suitability. Noted that Pseudoplatystoma species are among the most common large Amazonian catfishes misrepresented in trade. Confirmed active piscivore nature and rapid growth rate.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudoplatystoma fasciatum ecology and distribution
additional.notes: Ecology tab data: depth range, habitat (benthopelagic in large rivers and floodplains), migration patterns during Amazon flood pulse, aquaculture notes confirming hormone-induced commercial spawning. Tiger-banded coloration confirmed as species-typical pattern.

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. Commercial aquaculture uses hormone-induced spawning in large outdoor raceways; wild spawning is tied to Amazon flood-pulse seasonality. Captive home breeding is not feasible.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 90cm-1m+ in wild. Active piscivore. Sold tiny as ornamental but outgrows all home setups within months.
